title: Viburnum fuscum
intro: Viburnum fuscum, also known as Black Haw, is a deciduous shrub native to North America, characterized by its clusters of small white flowers and dark blue-black berries. The plant has been traditionally used in herbal medicine to treat various health issues, including fever, rheumatism, and menstrual disorders, due to its anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties. The bioactive constituents of Viburnum fuscum include viburnin, tannins, and essential oils, which are responsible for its medicinal effects. Black Haw is commonly prepared as a tea, tincture, or dried flower extract, and is often used in traditional herbal remedies to soothe menstrual cramps, reduce fever, and alleviate joint pain.
taxonomy: Viburnum fuscum is classified as a member of the family Adoxaceae, which is a family of flowering plants commonly known as the honeysuckle or elderberry family. This classification places Viburnum fuscum within the order Dipsacales, which includes other plant families such as Caprifoliaceae and Valerianaceae. Further classification of Viburnum fuscum is as a genus within the family Adoxaceae, sharing characteristics with other Viburnum species. At the level of kingdom, Viburnum fuscum is classified as Plantae, which includes all plant species. The specific classification of Viburnum fuscum is a part of its scientific nomenclature.
